heroOverview: Hypercalcemia is defined as a total serum calcium above 2.6 mmol/L (10.4 mg/dL) and is found in approximately 0.6% of the general population on routine screening. The two causes account for over 90% of all cases: primary hyperparathyroidism (most common in outpatients) and hypercalcemia of malignancy (most common in hospitalised patients). Calcium regulates vascular tone, gastrointestinal motility, neuromuscular function, and renal concentrating ability, which explains the classic symptom mnemonic 'bones, stones, groans, and psychic moans' — bone pain, kidney stones, nausea, and neuropsychiatric symptoms. Mild hypercalcemia is often discovered incidentally and progresses slowly over years; severe or rapidly rising calcium above 3.5 mmol/L constitutes a hypercalcemic crisis requiring emergency treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 0.6% of the general population; primary hyperparathyroidism is the most common cause in outpatients, occurring in 1–4 per 1,000 adults
keyStats.demographics: Primary hyperparathyroidism is 3 times more common in women than men; peak incidence in postmenopausal women aged 50–65; malignancy-related hypercalcemia peaks in older adults with solid tumours
keyStats.avgAge: Primary hyperparathyroidism peaks at 50–65 years; malignancy-related at 60–70 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 100,000 new cases of primary hyperparathyroidism diagnosed annually in the United States alone (NIDDK)

definition: Hypercalcemia (ICD-10: E83.52) is an electrolyte disorder characterised by a total serum calcium above 2.6 mmol/L or an ionised calcium above 1.35 mmol/L. As with hypocalcemia, the total calcium must be corrected for albumin because approximately 45% of serum calcium is protein-bound. The severity is graded as mild (2.6–2.9 mmol/L), moderate (2.9–3.4 mmol/L), or severe (above 3.4 mmol/L). The fundamental pathophysiology involves excessive calcium entry into the extracellular fluid from three sources — excess PTH-driven bone resorption and renal calcium retention, excessive calcitriol-mediated intestinal absorption, or humoral mediators from a tumour bypassing normal regulatory feedback. In primary hyperparathyroidism, a single parathyroid adenoma (80% of cases), multi-gland hyperplasia (15–20%), or rarely carcinoma autonomously over-secretes PTH. In malignancy, either direct osteolytic bone metastases or tumour-produced PTH-related protein (PTHrP) — a PTH analogue that the PTH assay does not detect — drives calcium out of bone. Sarcoidosis and other granulomatous diseases produce unregulated calcitriol from activated macrophages. The condition falls primarily under endocrinology (for primary hyperparathyroidism) and oncology (for malignancy-related causes).

typesClassification.description: Autonomous PTH overproduction from a parathyroid adenoma (80%) or hyperplasia; the most common form in outpatients, often mild and asymptomatic, discovered on routine bloods.

PTHrP-mediated humoral hypercalcemia from solid tumours (lung, breast, renal cell, head and neck) accounts for 80% of malignancy-related cases; osteolytic metastases (multiple myeloma, breast cancer) account for most of the remainder.

Activated macrophages in sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, and fungal granulomas produce unregulated 1-alpha-hydroxylase activity, converting vitamin D to calcitriol; this form is particularly exacerbated by sunlight and calcium-rich foods.
Excessive supplementation with vitamin D3 or its analogues raises calcitriol, driving intestinal calcium absorption far above normal; increasingly seen with very high-dose supplement protocols.

A benign autosomal dominant condition caused by a loss-of-function mutation in the calcium-sensing receptor gene; serum calcium is mildly elevated but urinary calcium is paradoxically low. Distinguishing FHH from primary hyperparathyroidism prevents unnecessary parathyroid surgery.

primarySymptoms: Polyuria and polydipsia (passing large volumes of dilute urine and feeling constantly thirsty) — hypercalcemia impairs the renal concentrating mechanism by downregulating aquaporin-2 channels in the collecting duct, producing a nephrogenic diabetes insipidus-like state.

Nausea, vomiting, and loss of appetite — calcium slows gastrointestinal motility; constipation and vague epigastric discomfort are among the most frequent complaints in outpatients with primary hyperparathyroidism.
Constipation — smooth muscle hyperpolarisation throughout the gastrointestinal tract slows transit; it is frequently the presenting complaint in mild-to-moderate hypercalcemia.
Fatigue and muscle weakness — hypercalcemia reduces neuromuscular excitability, causing the opposite of hypocalcemia; patients describe a heavy, generalised tiredness disproportionate to exertion.

Cognitive slowing, depression, and anxiety — calcium affects neuronal excitability and synaptic transmission; even mild chronic elevation is associated with impaired attention, low mood, and reduced quality of life.

Confusion and delirium — moderate-to-severe hypercalcemia (above 3.0–3.2 mmol/L) impairs consciousness; this may be the presenting feature in elderly patients and is often mistaken for primary dementia.

Renal colic and kidney stones — PTH-driven hypercalciuria increases the urinary calcium-to-creatinine ratio, promoting calcium oxalate and calcium phosphate stone formation; nephrolithiasis occurs in 15–20% of patients with primary hyperparathyroidism.

Bone pain and pathological fractures — PTH-mediated cortical bone resorption reduces bone mineral density at the distal third of the radius, hip, and lumbar spine; osteitis fibrosa cystica is the severe end of the spectrum, now rarely seen with early diagnosis.
Shortened QT interval on ECG — the opposite change from hypocalcemia; in severe or rapidly rising hypercalcemia, complete heart block or cardiac arrest can occur.
Pancreatitis — high calcium activates trypsinogen within the pancreas prematurely; both acute and recurrent pancreatitis are recognised complications of sustained hypercalcemia.
earlyWarningSigns: Kidney stones in an otherwise healthy adult, particularly in a postmenopausal woman — a one-in-three chance of finding primary hyperparathyroidism on calcium measurement.
Persistent constipation and vague nausea without a gastrointestinal cause identified on initial workup.
Low bone mineral density on a DEXA scan with an abnormal T-score at the distal radius, out of proportion to age.
Mild incidental elevation of calcium above 2.6 mmol/L on a routine blood panel with no symptoms — the most common way primary hyperparathyroidism is found.
emergencySigns: Calcium above 3.5 mmol/L with confusion, stupor, or coma — a hypercalcemic crisis requiring emergency IV saline and, where appropriate, bisphosphonate infusion.
Severe nausea and vomiting preventing oral fluid intake, which worsens hypercalcemia through dehydration and reduced urinary calcium excretion.
Acute short QT interval with widened QRS on ECG or new cardiac arrhythmia in the context of very high calcium.
Acute pancreatitis with rising calcium — a dangerous combination that can rapidly deteriorate.
Marked confusion or unconsciousness in a patient known to have cancer or elevated calcium.

causes.description: A single benign adenoma autonomously secretes PTH irrespective of serum calcium, driving PTH-mediated bone resorption and renal calcium retention simultaneously; it is the cause in more than 50% of all outpatient hypercalcemia cases and in up to 80% of asymptomatic incidentally discovered cases.

Tumours of the lung, breast, renal cell, head, neck, and bladder produce PTH-related protein, a structural PTH analogue that binds the PTH receptor and drives bone resorption and renal calcium retention at a rate that often exceeds primary hyperparathyroidism; it accounts for approximately 80% of malignancy-related cases.

Multiple myeloma plasma cells and breast cancer metastases directly activate osteoclasts in bone through RANK-L and other cytokines, releasing large quantities of skeletal calcium; the hypercalcemia tends to be moderate-to-severe and to appear alongside advanced disease.

Macrophages within granulomas express 1-alpha-hydroxylase outside normal feedback control, converting circulating 25-OH-D to calcitriol; this drives intestinal calcium hyperabsorption and the hypercalcemia is worsened by sun exposure and vitamin D supplementation.

Supplementation with very high doses of vitamin D3 (typically above 10,000–50,000 IU/day sustained over weeks) or its active analogues (calcitriol, alfacalcidol) raises calcitriol to supraphysiological levels, causing intestinal hyperabsorption of calcium that exceeds renal excretory capacity.

Thiazides reduce urinary calcium excretion by stimulating distal tubular calcium reabsorption; mild hypercalcemia occasionally results, particularly when superimposed on borderline primary hyperparathyroidism or another cause; it usually resolves within days of stopping the drug.

Inactivating mutations in the calcium-sensing receptor gene shift the calcium set-point upward, so the parathyroid and kidney behave as if calcium is normal despite elevated serum levels; the combination of mild hypercalcemia, low urinary calcium excretion, and a positive family history identifies this benign non-surgical cause.

riskFactors.description: Primary hyperparathyroidism is approximately 3 times more common in women than men and peaks in the decade after menopause; oestrogen withdrawal unmasks adenomas that were subclinical before.
Radiation to the neck region is a known risk factor for both sporadic and radiation-induced parathyroid adenoma and carcinoma; latency of 20–30 years is typical.

MEN-1 (MENIN gene mutation) and MEN-2A (RET gene mutation) are autosomal dominant syndromes in which multigland parathyroid hyperplasia causes primary hyperparathyroidism alongside pancreatic and pituitary (MEN-1) or medullary thyroid and phaeochromocytoma (MEN-2A) tumours.

riskFactors.factor: Certain cancers (lung, breast, multiple myeloma, renal cell)
riskFactors.description: These solid tumours are responsible for the great majority of hypercalcemia of malignancy through PTHrP secretion or osteolytic metastases; hypercalcemia in a cancer patient carries a median survival of weeks to months without treatment.
Active granulomatous disease produces unregulated calcitriol; risk increases with sun exposure and vitamin D supplementation, so dietary calcium and sun should be moderated in known sarcoidosis.
Thiazides reduce renal calcium excretion; stopping them resolves hypercalcemia in thiazide-induced cases within days, distinguishing it from a persistent cause.
Sustained supplementation above 4,000 IU/day can accumulate to toxicity levels; monitoring 25-OH-D and calcium at 3–6 month intervals prevents this entirely.

affectedDemographics: Adults with solid tumours — particularly lung cancer, breast cancer, and renal cell carcinoma
Patients with multiple myeloma or bone metastases
People with known sarcoidosis, especially those taking vitamin D supplements
Older adults on thiazide diuretics for hypertension
Families with MEN-1 or MEN-2A gene mutations
Adults using very high-dose vitamin D3 supplementation
diagnosisOverview: A serum calcium above 2.6 mmol/L (albumin-corrected) on two separate occasions confirms hypercalcemia. The first and most critical diagnostic step thereafter is measuring intact PTH. A high or high-normal PTH in the setting of hypercalcemia is strongly suggestive of primary hyperparathyroidism, since normal feedback would suppress PTH below 20 pg/mL when calcium is elevated. A low or undetectable PTH points toward a non-PTH-mediated cause — most commonly malignancy, granulomatous disease, or vitamin D toxicity. PTHrP is then measured to assess for humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy. Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D identifies vitamin D toxicity. Spot or 24-hour urinary calcium is essential: a low urine calcium (calcium-creatinine clearance ratio below 0.01) in mild hypercalcemia with high-normal PTH favours familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia over surgery-indicated primary hyperparathyroidism. In confirmed primary hyperparathyroidism, neck ultrasound and a sestamibi parathyroid scan localise the adenoma before surgery. A bone density DEXA scan (including the distal third of the radius) quantifies skeletal involvement. In malignancy-related hypercalcemia, imaging for the primary tumour and extent of metastases directs management. Renal function and urine calcium should be checked to identify nephrocalcinosis before the severity worsens.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms hypercalcemia and grades its severity; requires albumin correction or ionised calcium measurement for accuracy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine venous blood draw; result available within hours on any standard metabolic panel.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The pivotal test: elevated PTH confirms primary or tertiary hyperparathyroidism; suppressed PTH points to non-PTH-mediated causes (malignancy, vitamin D toxicity, granulomatous disease).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Drawn from the same sample as calcium; PTH is labile and should be transported cold to preserve accuracy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy when PTH is suppressed and no other cause is apparent.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood draw sent to a specialised laboratory; some assays require specific transport conditions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies vitamin D toxicity and is also used to assess granulomatous disease-associated hypercalcemia (where 25-OH-D may be normal but 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D is elevated).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venous blood draw; result in 1–2 days.

diagnosticTests.test: 24-hour urine calcium and creatinine (calcium-creatinine clearance ratio)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (very low urine calcium, ratio below 0.01) from primary hyperparathyroidism (normal-to-high urine calcium), preventing unnecessary parathyroid surgery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: All urine collected into a container over 24 hours; serum creatinine drawn on the same day.

diagnosticTests.test: Sestamibi parathyroid scan and neck ultrasound
diagnosticTests.purpose: Localises the adenoma in confirmed primary hyperparathyroidism to guide minimally invasive parathyroidectomy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sestamibi is a nuclear medicine scan (1–3 hours); ultrasound is painless and takes 15–20 minutes. Done together before surgery.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies cortical bone loss at the distal one-third radius — the site most sensitive to PTH-mediated resorption — as a criterion for surgical intervention in asymptomatic primary hyperparathyroidism.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A low-dose X-ray scan of the lumbar spine, hip, and wrist; painless and takes about 10 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Treatment depends on the cause, severity, and pace of calcium rise. Acute severe hypercalcemia (calcium above 3.0–3.5 mmol/L) or hypercalcemic crisis is initially treated with aggressive intravenous 0.9% saline hydration, typically 200–500 mL/hour, to restore intravascular volume and promote urinary calcium excretion — furosemide is no longer routinely added. In malignancy-related or granulomatous hypercalcemia, an intravenous bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid 4 mg over 15 minutes, or pamidronate 60–90 mg over 2 hours) is added once the patient is adequately hydrated; it reduces bone resorption over 2–4 days and lowers calcium for 2–4 weeks per infusion. Denosumab (120 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks) is an alternative in patients with renal impairment where bisphosphonates are less safe. Calcitonin (4 IU/kg IM or SC every 12 hours) acts fastest — lowering calcium within 4–6 hours — but tachyphylaxis develops within 48 hours so it is used as a bridge while bisphosphonates take effect. Glucocorticoids (prednisolone 40–60 mg daily) effectively treat hypercalcemia from vitamin D toxicity, sarcoidosis, and haematological malignancies by reducing intestinal calcium absorption and calcitriol production. For primary hyperparathyroidism, parathyroidectomy is the only curative treatment; it is indicated for symptomatic disease and for asymptomatic patients meeting at least one criterion from consensus guidelines: calcium more than 0.25 mmol/L above the upper limit of normal, eGFR below 60 mL/min, DEXA T-score below −2.5 at any site, age below 50 years, or 24-hour urine calcium above 400 mg/day. Minimally invasive parathyroidectomy guided by preoperative localisation achieves cure in over 95% of patients. Cinacalcet, a calcimimetic that allosterically activates the calcium-sensing receptor and suppresses PTH secretion, is used for patients unsuitable for or declining surgery and for parathyroid carcinoma. Chronic mild hypercalcemia not requiring urgent intervention is managed with adequate hydration, avoidance of thiazide diuretics and calcium supplements, and regular monitoring.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous 0.9% saline hydration
medicalTreatments.description: The cornerstone of acute hypercalcemia management; vigorous IV hydration (200–500 mL/hour) restores renal perfusion and promotes calciuresis — calcium is excreted as the glomerular filtration rate rises. Contraindicated in patients with severe heart failure or anuria.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Lowers calcium by approximately 0.25–0.75 mmol/L within 12–24 hours; provides stabilisation while definitive therapy takes effect.

medicalTreatments.name: Zoledronic acid (IV bisphosphonate)
medicalTreatments.description: 4 mg IV over 15 minutes (with prior hydration); inhibits osteoclast-mediated bone resorption and is the most potent bisphosphonate for acute hypercalcemia of malignancy. Dose is adjusted for eGFR; renal function should be checked before each dose.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalises calcium in approximately 70–90% of patients with hypercalcemia of malignancy within 4–10 days; effect lasts 2–4 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Pamidronate (IV bisphosphonate)
medicalTreatments.description: 60–90 mg IV over 2–4 hours; an alternative to zoledronic acid, used in some centres for patients with moderate renal impairment; response is slower but comparable at 7 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves normocalcemia in approximately 60–80% of malignancy-related hypercalcemia within one week.

medicalTreatments.name: Calcitonin
medicalTreatments.description: 4 IU/kg IM or SC every 12 hours; the fastest-acting agent, reducing calcium within 4–6 hours by inhibiting osteoclast activity and increasing urinary calcium excretion; tachyphylaxis limits use beyond 48 hours so it is used only as a bridge therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces calcium by approximately 0.3–0.5 mmol/L in the first 24 hours in most patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Cinacalcet
medicalTreatments.description: An oral calcimimetic that activates the parathyroid calcium-sensing receptor, suppressing PTH secretion; used in primary hyperparathyroidism when surgery is not possible, in parathyroid carcinoma, and in secondary hyperparathyroidism of CKD.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces calcium by approximately 0.5 mmol/L and normalises calcium in about 75% of primary hyperparathyroidism patients who are not surgical candidates.

medicalTreatments.name: Glucocorticoids (prednisolone)
medicalTreatments.description: 40–60 mg prednisolone daily is effective specifically for vitamin D toxicity, sarcoidosis, lymphoma, and other granulomatous causes; they reduce intestinal calcium absorption by inhibiting calcitriol production from granuloma macrophages.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalises calcium within 2–5 days in approximately 75–80% of patients with granulomatous or vitamin D-related hypercalcemia.

surgicalOptions.name: Minimally invasive parathyroidectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the identified parathyroid adenoma through a small incision using intraoperative PTH monitoring (which drops by more than 50% within 10 minutes of adenoma excision, confirming cure); the preferred approach when preoperative localisation is successful.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure rate above 95% in experienced centres with confirmed single-gland disease on imaging.

surgicalOptions.name: Bilateral neck exploration with four-gland identification
surgicalOptions.description: Traditional approach used when preoperative localisation fails or multigland disease (hyperplasia) is suspected; allows all four glands to be inspected. Typically 3.5 glands are removed in four-gland hyperplasia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term cure rate approximately 93–97% for multi-gland disease in experienced parathyroid surgical centres.

alternativeTreatments.name: Hydration and dietary modification
alternativeTreatments.description: Drinking 2–3 litres of water daily maintains urinary calcium excretion and reduces stone formation risk; avoiding calcium-rich foods and excessive vitamin D supplementation is appropriate while awaiting definitive treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Avoidance of exacerbating factors
alternativeTreatments.description: Stopping thiazide diuretics (which reduce renal calcium excretion), calcium-containing antacids, and vitamin D supplements allows calcium to fall in borderline or drug-precipitated cases without pharmacological intervention.

whySeeSpecialist: An endocrinologist should be involved as soon as primary hyperparathyroidism or unexplained hypercalcemia is confirmed, both to confirm the diagnosis and to assess whether surgical criteria are met. A parathyroid surgeon experienced in minimally invasive techniques should see all surgical candidates — outcomes are strongly operator-dependent, with cure rates above 95% in high-volume centres. In malignancy-related hypercalcemia, the oncology team directs treatment of the underlying cancer while the endocrinologist or nephrologist manages acute calcium control.
doctorSelectionGuide: For primary hyperparathyroidism, look for a surgeon who performs a minimum of 50 parathyroid operations annually and who uses intraoperative PTH monitoring; cure rates and complication rates differ substantially between low- and high-volume operators. Ask about the centre's localisation protocol (sestamibi plus ultrasound versus 4D-CT). For medical management of hypercalcemia, an endocrinologist with a specific calcium and bone-mineral interest is preferable to a generalist.
preventionStrategies: Screen calcium routinely in patients with nephrolithiasis — up to one-third will have primary hyperparathyroidism.
In patients with sarcoidosis, limit sun exposure and avoid vitamin D supplements; monitor calcium every 6–12 months.
Avoid sustained very high-dose vitamin D supplementation (above 4,000 IU/day) without measuring 25-OH-D and serum calcium at 3–6 month intervals.
In patients with known MEN-1 or MEN-2A, begin annual calcium and PTH screening from the second decade of life.
Monitor calcium and renal function 3 months after starting denosumab or bisphosphonates, particularly in patients with vitamin D insufficiency.
Review and if possible stop thiazide diuretics in patients with borderline or confirmed hypercalcemia.
lifestyleModifications: Drink at least 2–3 litres of water daily to maintain high urine flow and prevent kidney stone formation.
Avoid calcium supplements unless specifically prescribed; dietary calcium at recommended intake levels is safe but high-dose supplementation is not.
Limit vitamin D supplementation to standard maintenance doses (600–2,000 IU/day) unless higher doses are medically supervised.
If you have sarcoidosis, limit sun exposure and notify your doctor before taking any vitamin D product.
Avoid prolonged bed rest or immobilisation, which increases bone resorption and worsens hypercalcemia.
Attend regular blood test monitoring appointments and report new confusion, severe thirst, or stone pain promptly.

recommended: Adequate daily fluid intake — water is the single most important dietary measure to maintain urinary calcium excretion and prevent stones
A moderate calcium intake through normal diet (700–1,000 mg/day) rather than supplements, which carries less hypercalcemia risk than equivalent supplement doses
High-fibre foods to reduce constipation, a common symptom of hypercalcemia
avoid: Very high-dose calcium supplements (above 1,000 mg elemental calcium per day) unless prescribed, which add to the circulating calcium load
High-dose vitamin D supplements or calcitriol unless supervised — particularly relevant in granulomatous disease where even modest supplementation can precipitate a crisis
Excessive salt, which increases urinary calcium excretion and can worsen stone risk
Dehydration — even mild volume depletion reduces GFR and dramatically increases calcium levels

exerciseGuidelines: Regular weight-bearing exercise is encouraged and helps to maintain bone density in primary hyperparathyroidism, slowing the cortical bone loss that PTH drives. Immobilisation and bed rest are actively harmful — mobilising calcium from bone worsens hypercalcemia. During acute severe hypercalcemia (calcium above 3.2 mmol/L), strenuous exercise is avoided until calcium is controlled, because vigorous activity in a dehydrated, hypercalcemic patient risks arrhythmia. Once calcium is normalised, there are no activity restrictions.
dailyManagement: Keep well hydrated throughout the day — pass pale urine as a guide; darker urine indicates inadequate fluid intake and risks worsening calcium.
Take any prescribed cinacalcet with food to reduce nausea.
Report new or worsening confusion, nausea, or kidney stone pain to your clinician — these suggest calcium is rising.
Attend scheduled calcium and renal function checks every 3–12 months depending on severity.
Inform all prescribers and pharmacists of your hypercalcemia so that calcium supplements and thiazide diuretics are avoided.
If awaiting parathyroid surgery, follow activity and diet advice strictly and seek emergency care if you develop severe symptoms before your operation date.
prognosis: Prognosis depends almost entirely on the underlying cause. Surgically cured primary hyperparathyroidism has an excellent long-term outlook: calcium normalises immediately, bone density recovers substantially over 1–3 years, and the risk of nephrolithiasis falls. Patients managed conservatively (not meeting surgical criteria) have a stable course in roughly 75% over 10 years. Malignancy-related hypercalcemia carries a much graver prognosis driven by the underlying cancer — median survival after diagnosis of hypercalcemia of malignancy has been reported at 30 days in some series, though modern oncological treatment has improved this in responsive tumours. Sarcoidosis-related and vitamin D toxicity-related hypercalcemia resolve with treatment of the underlying condition. Untreated severe hypercalcemia is potentially fatal from cardiac arrhythmia, pancreatitis, or renal failure.
recoveryTimeline: IV saline stabilises acute hypercalcemia within 12–24 hours. Bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid) normalise calcium over 4–10 days and the effect lasts 3–4 weeks. After parathyroidectomy, calcium normalises within 24–48 hours in most patients; a transient post-operative hypocalcemia (hungry bone syndrome) lasting days to weeks is managed with calcium and calcitriol. Bone density recovery after curative surgery takes 1–3 years. Cinacalcet lowers calcium within 2–4 weeks of dose titration.
complications: Nephrolithiasis (kidney stones) — calcium oxalate and calcium phosphate stones form when urinary calcium excretion exceeds the renal handling threshold; they occur in 15–20% of patients with primary hyperparathyroidism and can cause obstructive uropathy.

Nephrocalcinosis and chronic kidney disease — prolonged hypercalciuria deposits calcium in renal tubules, progressively impairing kidney function; eGFR below 60 mL/min is a criterion for parathyroid surgery.
Osteoporosis and pathological fractures — sustained PTH-mediated cortical bone resorption reduces bone mineral density, particularly at the distal radius and hip, raising fragility fracture risk.

Cardiac arrhythmia — a shortened QT interval and hyperpolarisation of cardiac membranes can cause conduction abnormalities; very high calcium (above 4.0 mmol/L) may cause ventricular fibrillation or cardiac arrest.
Pancreatitis — acute and chronic pancreatitis are recognised complications of sustained hypercalcemia; calcium activates trypsinogen and damages pancreatic acinar cells.
Hypercalcemic crisis — calcium above 3.5 mmol/L with altered consciousness, vomiting, and dehydration is a medical emergency with significant mortality if untreated.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Primary Hyperparathyroidism
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of primary hyperparathyroidism, its symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

supportResources.name: Norman Parathyroid Center — Hypercalcemia Information
supportResources.description: High-volume parathyroid surgical centre patient education resource on hypercalcemia causes, symptoms, and the role of parathyroid surgery.

supportResources.name: NHS — Hypercalcaemia
supportResources.description: Plain-English UK overview of high blood calcium, its causes, symptoms, and when to seek care.

sources.title: Bilezikian JP et al. Guidelines for the management of asymptomatic primary hyperparathyroidism. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism, 2022
Stewart AF. Hypercalcemia associated with cancer. New England Journal of Medicine, 2005
Shane E, Irani D. Hypercalcemia: pathogenesis, clinical manifestations, differential diagnosis and management. In: Primer on the Metabolic Bone Diseases, ASBMR, 2008
Marcocci C, Cetani F. Clinical practice: Primary hyperparathyroidism. New England Journal of Medicine, 2011
Peacock M. Primary hyperparathyroidism and the kidney. Endocrinology and Metabolism Clinics of North America, 2014

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary hyperparathyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary hyperparathyroidism is the most common cause of outpatient hypercalcemia — the two terms are often conflated but hyperparathyroidism is the upstream diagnosis while hypercalcemia is the laboratory finding it produces.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypocalcemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypocalcemia and hypercalcemia cause opposite neurological pictures — tetany and cramps versus weakness and confusion; a calcium level immediately distinguishes them, but both can initially present with non-specific fatigue.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypothyroidism produces fatigue, constipation, and cognitive slowing that closely resembles mild hypercalcemia; thyroid function tests and a calcium level are needed to separate the two, and thyroid disease can itself slightly raise calcium.

relatedConditions.name: Primary hyperparathyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common cause of outpatient hypercalcemia; a parathyroid adenoma drives sustained calcium elevation that cures with surgery.

relatedConditions.name: Hypocalcemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Opposite end of calcium dysregulation; treatment of hypercalcemia can overshoot into hypocalcemia, particularly post-parathyroidectomy hungry bone syndrome.

relatedConditions.name: Kidney stones
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypercalciuria from sustained hypercalcemia is a major driver of calcium oxalate and calcium phosphate kidney stone formation.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoporosis
relatedConditions.relevance: PTH-mediated cortical bone resorption in primary hyperparathyroidism causes low bone density at the distal radius and hip; parathyroidectomy partially reverses this.

faqs.question: What is hypercalcemia?
faqs.answer: Hypercalcemia means your blood calcium level is above normal (above 2.6 mmol/L). Excess calcium affects muscles, the gastrointestinal tract, kidneys, and the brain, causing weakness, nausea, constipation, kidney stones, and confusion.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of high calcium?
faqs.answer: The classic symptoms are muscle weakness, fatigue, nausea, constipation, increased thirst, frequent urination, and confusion. The mnemonic used by doctors is 'bones, stones, groans, and psychic moans' — bone pain, kidney stones, stomach upset, and mood changes.

faqs.question: What causes hypercalcemia?
faqs.answer: Over 90% of cases are caused by either primary hyperparathyroidism (a benign parathyroid adenoma overproducing PTH) or hypercalcemia of malignancy (cancer releasing a PTH-like protein or destroying bone). Other causes include sarcoidosis and vitamin D toxicity.

faqs.question: How is hypercalcemia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: A blood calcium level confirms the diagnosis. The next step is measuring intact PTH: high PTH suggests a parathyroid adenoma; suppressed PTH points toward cancer, sarcoidosis, or vitamin D excess. Urine calcium, vitamin D level, and PTHrP complete the workup.

faqs.question: Is hypercalcemia serious?
faqs.answer: Mild hypercalcemia (just above normal) is often asymptomatic and stable for years. Severe hypercalcemia above 3.5 mmol/L — particularly when caused by cancer — is a medical emergency that can cause coma, heart arrhythmia, or kidney failure if not treated urgently.

faqs.question: How is hypercalcemia treated?
faqs.answer: Acute severe hypercalcemia is treated with IV saline to increase urinary calcium excretion, followed by bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid) to reduce bone resorption. Sarcoidosis-related cases respond to steroids. Primary hyperparathyroidism is cured by parathyroid surgery.

faqs.question: What is primary hyperparathyroidism?
faqs.answer: Primary hyperparathyroidism is the most common cause of outpatient hypercalcemia. A benign tumour (adenoma) in one of the four parathyroid glands produces too much PTH, driving calcium out of bone and into the blood. Surgery removes the adenoma and cures the condition in over 95% of cases.

faqs.question: Can cancer cause high calcium?
faqs.answer: Yes. Several cancers — particularly lung, breast, kidney, and head and neck cancers — produce a protein called PTHrP that mimics PTH and drives calcium out of bone. Multiple myeloma destroys bone directly. Hypercalcemia in a cancer patient is often a sign of advanced disease.

faqs.question: When does hypercalcemia need surgery?
faqs.answer: Parathyroid surgery is recommended for primary hyperparathyroidism when calcium is more than 0.25 mmol/L above normal, when kidney function has fallen, when bone density is low (T-score below −2.5), when the patient is under 50, or when symptoms are present. Minimally invasive surgery cures more than 95% of patients.

faqs.question: What is cinacalcet and who takes it?
faqs.answer: Cinacalcet is an oral medication that lowers PTH and calcium by activating the calcium-sensing receptor in the parathyroid gland. It is used for patients with primary hyperparathyroidism who cannot have surgery, for parathyroid cancer, and for secondary hyperparathyroidism in kidney disease.

faqs.question: Can vitamin D supplements cause high calcium?
faqs.answer: Yes, if taken in very high doses (typically above 10,000–50,000 IU/day for weeks or months). Standard doses of 600–2,000 IU/day are safe for most people. Vitamin D toxicity-related hypercalcemia responds quickly to stopping the supplement and taking glucocorticoids.

faqs.question: Can sarcoidosis cause high calcium?
faqs.answer: Yes. Activated immune cells in sarcoidosis granulomas produce large amounts of calcitriol (active vitamin D), which drives intestinal calcium absorption far above normal. Hypercalcemia in sarcoidosis is worsened by sun exposure and vitamin D supplements, and it responds well to corticosteroids.

faqs.question: What is a hypercalcemic crisis?
faqs.answer: A hypercalcemic crisis is calcium above 3.5 mmol/L causing altered consciousness, severe vomiting, extreme thirst, and dehydration. It is a medical emergency treated with aggressive IV saline in hospital, followed by bisphosphonate infusion and treatment of the underlying cause.

faqs.question: Does high calcium cause kidney stones?
faqs.answer: Yes. High blood calcium drives excess calcium into the urine. When urinary calcium exceeds the kidney's handling capacity, calcium combines with oxalate or phosphate to form kidney stones. Nephrolithiasis occurs in about 15–20% of patients with primary hyperparathyroidism.

faqs.question: Does hypercalcemia cause osteoporosis?
faqs.answer: Yes, in primary hyperparathyroidism. Excess PTH preferentially resorbs cortical bone at the forearm and hip, reducing bone density and raising fracture risk. Parathyroid surgery partially reverses this bone loss over 1–3 years after cure.

faqs.question: Can hypercalcemia cause heart problems?
faqs.answer: Yes. High calcium shortens the QT interval on an ECG and can cause heart conduction abnormalities. In severe or rapidly rising hypercalcemia, cardiac arrest can occur. This is why ECG monitoring is part of the management of any moderate-to-severe hypercalcemia.

faqs.question: How long does it take to correct high calcium?
faqs.answer: IV saline stabilises acute hypercalcemia within 12–24 hours. Bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid) normalise calcium over 4–10 days. Parathyroid surgery normalises calcium within 24–48 hours. Cinacalcet typically lowers calcium within 2–4 weeks of dose titration.

faqs.question: Is hypercalcemia hereditary?
faqs.answer: Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (FHH) is an autosomal dominant inherited condition causing mild lifelong hypercalcemia that does not need surgery. Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN-1) and type 2A also cause familial hyperparathyroidism. Genetic testing identifies these conditions.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with high calcium?
faqs.answer: Mild hypercalcemia is not a bar to regular weight-bearing exercise, which is actually encouraged because it helps maintain bone density. During acute severe hypercalcemia, strenuous activity is avoided until calcium is controlled, as dehydration and exertion can worsen the condition.

faqs.question: What should I avoid eating with hypercalcemia?
faqs.answer: Avoid high-dose calcium supplements and vitamin D supplements unless prescribed. Stay well hydrated — dehydration dramatically worsens hypercalcemia. Limit high-salt foods, which increase urinary calcium excretion. A normal calcium diet is safe; restriction is not routinely recommended.

metaDescription: Hypercalcemia (high calcium) causes weakness, nausea, confusion and kidney stones. Learn causes including hyperparathyroidism and cancer. Doctor-reviewed.
